turns.content: I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept app and I want to avoid hardcoding environment variables or file paths. How does the config layering approach with figment help me handle different profiles like dev, prod, and test without writing separate code for each?

That's a great question because it touches on why we care about separation of concerns in configuration. With figment, you define your settings as a single set of structs that implement the Config trait, then layer different sources like environment variables and files into profiles using the Builder pattern. This means you don't need to write separate logic for each profile; instead, you just swap out which source is active when you call build() on the config builder.
So does that mean I can define all my structs once and reuse them across profiles? What happens if a value isn't found in one layer?

Exactly, you define your structs once and let figment handle the rest. If a value is missing from the current profile's sources, it automatically falls back to the next lower layer or uses defaults you've specified in your struct. This makes testing much easier since you can run with just environment variables for dev, then switch to files for staging without changing any logic.

I'm trying to set up a config system for my new service using figment, but I'm confused about how the profiles actually interact. Specifically, if I have environment variables and a TOML file, does the env always override the file?

The order of layers matters because figment processes them sequentially from defaults up to the current profile. You can define multiple profiles in your config like production or development, and each layer sits on top of the previous one so environment variables will override values found in the file if they share the same key. Think of it like a layered cake where each ingredient is added on top of the last, so the final taste depends on which layers you eat first.
That analogy helps but I still don't understand how to extract these values into my structs. If I define a struct with env and file fields, will figment automatically fill them?

You need to create a custom source that implements the Source trait for your struct so figment knows how to parse it. The standard library provides a Json wrapper but you'll likely need to write your own implementation using serde_json::Value or derive the Deserialize trait manually if the config format isn't JSON.
